data.title: Steel Rises Amid Softer Post-Parade Recovery
data.description:
Steel rebar futures climbed above CNY 3,060 per tonne on Friday, rebounding from eight-week lows as Chinese mills restarted production after recent output curbs tied to the military parade.

Still, the recovery in output lagged expectations, with markets looking for a stronger rebound as mills resumed operations on Thursday.

At the same time, inventories of major steel products have been building since mid-August, underscoring weak demand from the prolonged property slump.

The stockpile overhang continues to weigh on prices and could limit mills’ willingness to ramp up production.

In corporate news, Nippon Steel said Wednesday it has withdrawn a civil lawsuit filed in the US over its planned acquisition of United States Steel.

Both firms stated Thursday that they will “remain focused on steelmaking and collective bargaining.”

data.title: Hungary Industrial Output Rebounds in July
data.description:
Industrial production in Hungary rose by 1% year-on-year in July 2025, rebounding from a 4.9% drop in the previous month, preliminary estimates showed.

This marked the first month of growth in industrial activity since March, mainly driven by increased production of vehicles, food, beverages and tobacco products, and computer, electronic and optical products.

On the other hand, output declined for electrical equipment.

On a seasonally adjusted monthly basis, industrial activity advanced by 2% in July, the highest since October 2024 and recovering from a 1.2% decrease in the preceding period.

Considering the first seven months of the year, industrial output was 3.5% higher compared to the same period a year earlier.

data.title: Safran Explores Sale of Part of Aircraft Interior Unit, Financial Times Says, Citing Sources
data.description:
• France's Safran is in the early stages of exploring options to sell part of its aircraft interior business, its smallest division, the Financial Times reports, citing unnamed sources.
  • The aerospace and defense company is exploring a disposal of assets that could be worth as much as 1.5 billion euros, the FT reports. Safran's cabin seat business, which is part of the same division, isn't for sale.
  • Safran is separately exploring the sale of its in-flight entertainment products, the FT reports.
  • Safran declined to comment to the FT.

Full story: https://tinyurl.com/3ux2auh5

Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com

data.title: Minesto kicks off microgrid project with partners and adds local end-user applications
data.description:
Minesto AB

Together with project partners Sev, Capture Energy and IVL, Minesto completed a planning and project set-up workshop for the 25 MSEK Microgrid Project awarded by Swedish Energy Agency. In parallel with the project, Minesto has added an integration of two local end-user applications of tidal energy in the Faroe Islands to strengthen customer involvement.

The session was conducted at Minesto's head office in Göteborg. With the 1,5 days planning meeting completed, Minesto and partners have laid a good foundation to deliver a microgrid turnkey installation in Vestmanna, Faroe Islands.

"We are off to a good start delivering a tidal energy microgrid system with the capacity to offer base-load renewable energy to both local needs in the Faroe Islands and further to millions of users on a global market," said Johannes Hüffmeier, COO of Minesto.

In parallel, work with integration of user applications is initiated together with Sev and local end users with the ambition to increase renewable energy content. Potential users in Vestmanna are in focus, with EV charging and an industrial process as the two prioritized areas for reduction of fossil energy consumption via electrification.

"Right at the start, we want to add delivery of tidal energy into electrification applications for the customer value of our microgrid to be proven and demonstrated. Small-scale electrification to reduce CO2 emissions is a core component in the global energy transition as well as in our sales growth projections," said Dr Martin Edlund, CEO of Minesto.

For additional information, please contact

Cecilia Sernhage, Chief Communications Officer

+46 735 23 71 58

ir@minesto.com

About Minesto

Minesto is a leading marine energy technology company with the mission to minimise the global carbon footprint of the energy industry by enabling plannable commercial power production from the ocean.

Minesto's award winning and patented product is the only verified marine power plant that operates cost efficiently in areas with low-flow tidal streams and ocean currents.

Minesto was founded in 2007 and has operations in Sweden, the Faroe Islands, Wales and Taiwan. The major shareholders in Minesto are BGA Invest and Corespring New Technology. The Minesto share (MINEST) is traded on Nasdaq First North Growth Market. Certified Adviser is G&W Fondkommission.

Read more about Minesto at www.minesto.com

Press images and other media material is available for download via minesto.com/media

Financial information including reports, prospectuses and company descriptions is available in Swedish at www.minesto.com/investor.

https://news.cision.com/minesto-ab/r/minesto-kicks-off-microgrid-project-with-partners-and-adds-local-end-user-applications%2Cc4230271

Notes:

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.

10.

data.title: Trump's wind war clouds Orsted's $9.4 billion survival plan
data.description:
Key points:
  • Orsted shareholders to vote on $9.4 billion rights issue
  • Group's two US projects at risk in Trump's war on wind power
  • S&P Global warns equity raise offers limited relief from credit pressures
  • Orsted's shares down 85% from 2021 peak

Danish offshore wind developer Orsted cut its 2025 profit outlook on Friday, just hours before it is poised to secure shareholder approval for a $9.4 billion emergency rights issue as it seeks to avert a looming credit downgrade.

Once celebrated as a trailblazer in offshore wind, the state-controlled group now finds itself in dire straits, grappling with industry-wide challenges as risks pile up on its U.S. projects due to President Donald Trump's opposition to wind power.

Adding to its troubles, low wind speeds in July and August and a delay to a project under construction off Taiwan prompted it on Friday to cut its operating profit outlook for 2025.

The stakes are high for the Danish firm, which has transformed itself from oil producer DONG Energy into a global renewables leader, its market value increasing fivefold between its 2016 IPO and a 2021 peak.

But supply chain disruptions, surging interest rates, project delays, and Trump’s anti-wind policies have battered the offshore wind sector, sending Orsted’s shares tumbling by 85% from their peak.

At the heart of the drama are Orsted’s U.S. projects Sunrise Wind and Revolution Wind, both of which have been thrown into uncertainty.

Two-thirds of the fresh capital it is seeking to raise is earmarked for Sunrise Wind, a project that saw potential co-investors flee after the Trump administration ordered Equinor to halt a neighboring wind farm in April.

U.S. officials also issued a stop-work order for the nearly completed Revolution Wind facility last month, prompting the joint venture running the project to file a lawsuit against the Trump administration over the decision.

The rights issue is critical for Orsted’s survival and its ability to retain its credit rating.

"We do this to ensure that we can continue to lead the expansion of offshore wind in our core markets here in Europe for the critical years to come," CEO Rasmus Errboe told reporters at a meeting of EU countries' energy ministers in Copenhagen on Thursday.

Ratings agency S&P Global warned that the equity raise might only buy the company three to six months of relief from construction delays before facing additional credit pressures.

S&P already downgraded Orsted to BBB- in August, the lowest investment-grade rating. Any further downgrade would push it into junk territory - a label that would impact its ability to finance future projects.

Norwegian state-controlled energy firm Equinor, a 10% shareholder in Orsted, has thrown a lifeline, pledging to inject up to 6 billion crowns ($941.2 million) into the rights issue.

Equinor CFO Torgrim Reitan described the moment as a crucial juncture. "We find it important to be a long-term and supportive investor in a period like this," Reitan said, hinting at a deeper strategic collaboration between the two companies.

The extraordinary general meeting is scheduled to begin at 0700 GMT in Copenhagen. The rights issue will likely be approved with a two-thirds majority.

($1 = 6.3751 Danish crowns)

data.title: Putin says any Western troops in Ukraine would be legitimate targets
data.description:
Russian President Vladimir Putin said on Friday that any Western troops deployed to Ukraine would be legitimate targets for Moscow to attack.

Putin was speaking a day after French President Emmanuel Macron said 26 countries had pledged to provide postwar security guarantees to Ukraine, including an international force on land, sea and in the air.

Russia has long argued that one of its reasons for going to war in Ukraine was to prevent NATO from admitting Kyiv as a member and placing its forces in Ukraine.

"Therefore, if some troops appear there, especially now, during military operations, we proceed from the fact that these will be legitimate targets for destruction," Putin told an economic forum in Vladivostok.

"And if decisions are reached that lead to peace, to long-term peace, then I simply do not see any sense in their presence on the territory of Ukraine, full stop."

data.title: Tullow Oil names industry veteran Ian Perks as new chief
data.description:
West Africa-focused Tullow Oil named oil and gas industry veteran Ian Perks as its new CEO on Friday, succeeding Richard Miller, who will return to his previous role as the group's chief financial officer.

The change comes as the company, which has been streamlining its operations, recently agreed to sell its assets in Gabon and Kenya to reduce debt.

Perks brings more than three decades of experience in the oil and gas sector, with a strong focus on Africa and international markets, having held senior positions at BG Group, Anadarko, and Total.

He has overseen major projects such as the $20 billion Mozambique LNG project and the delivery and subsequent operations of the $10 billion Queensland Gas Company LNG project, Tullow said in a statement.

In August, Tullow swung to an interim loss and cut its annual production forecast, citing continued underperformance at its flagship Jubilee oilfiled.

Miller was appointed as Tullow's interim chief in February after Rahul Dhir announced his exit as CEO last December.

data.title: Peel Hunt Expects Results to Beat Views as Activity Picks Up
data.description:
By Elena Vardon

Peel Hunt now expects fiscal 2026 results to current beat market expectations after experiencing a strong start to the year on a pick up in activity.

The U.K. mid- and small-cap specialist investment bank said Friday that its investment-banking arm has acted on several substantial M&A transactions so far this year and has supported clients on equity raises and block trades as activity in the equity-capital market starts to show signs of recovery.

The group added that its execution-services business is also generating encouraging revenue which is materially ahead of the comparable period a year prior.

Peel Hunt didn't provide market expectations for the year ending March 31. According to LSEG Workspace estimates, analysts anticipate 3.8 million pounds in pretax profit on 99.8 million pounds in revenue for the year. For fiscal 2025, it reported a pretax loss of 3.5 million pounds on 91.3 million pounds in revenue.

Revenue figures for the first six months of fiscal 2026 are set to be published on Oct. 1.

Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com

data.title: Kospi Gains 0.6% for the Week
Hang Seng Tech Index Last Up 2.0% at 5692.16
Eurozone Bond Yields Fall, Tracking Treasurys Ahead of U.S. Jobs Data — Market Talk
data.description:
0633 GMT - Eurozone government bond yields fall, tracking the move of U.S. Treasury yields and reflecting expctations of a further cooling U.S. labor market. U.S. payrolls are due at 1230 GMT, with analysts forecasting the addition of 75,000 jobs in August. Weak data is expected to cement interest-rate cut expectations by the Federal Reserve this month. "A September rate cut is likely a done deal," says J.P.Morgan Asset Management's Max McKechnie in a note. "Today's jobs report is more about the Federal Reserve's path from there," the global market strategist says. Eurozone 10-year bond yields drop by up to 4 basis points, with the 10-year German Bund yield falling 2.3 basis points to 2.701%, according to Tradeweb. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield falls 1 basis point to 3.580%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0630 GMT - The dollar falls as markets await the highly-anticipated U.S. nonfarm payrolls report later for hints on the potential pace of expected Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts. Economists in a WSJ survey expect the report at 1230 GMT to show payrolls rose 75,000 in August. The unemployment rate is expected to rise to 4.3% in August from 4.2% previously and wage growth is forecast to slow to 3.8% year-on-year. With immigration slowing, modest jobs growth doesn't necessarily indicate a loosening labor market, J.P. Morgan Asset Management strategist Max McKechnie says in a note. "Instead, investors should focus on the unemployment rate and wage growth for a clearer sense of the Fed's next steps." The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.113. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0625 GMT - The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield "wants" to have a go at 4% amid expectations of a weak payroll outcome, ING's Padhraic Garvey and Benjamin Schroeder say in a note. August payrolls data due at 1230 GMT, could fortify expectations of a Federal Reserve interest-rate cut this month. "The 10-year [yield] looks to have 4% as a level to aim for, depending on the payrolls number," the rates strategists say. However, a job addition data below 50,000 would likely to be needed to generate that type of market reaction, they say. "The closer to zero [in job additions] the more likely a 4% test becomes," they say. Analysts in a WSJ poll expect 75,000 jobs added in August. The 10-year yield falls 2.3 basis points to 4.152%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0605 GMT - A Trump executive order locks in a 15% base tariff rate for Japan, clearing some uncertainty but leaving key questions unanswered, says Stefan Angrick at Moody's Analytics. The order settles some doubts over car duties and cements Japan's pledge to buy more U.S. rice. But investment provisions remain murky, the economist says. Japan will funnel $550 billion into the U.S., though the composition is unclear. U.S. Commerce Secretary Lutnick insists it's real funding, while Tokyo has said the figure includes loans, guarantees, and equity via government-backed institutions, with direct equity accounting for just 1% to 2%, Angrick says. Economically, tariffs remain punishingly high. They are undercutting manufacturing and exports, and will trim at least half a percentage point from GDP. Politically, the deal offers scant relief for beleaguered PM Ishiba. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0557 GMT - Southeast Asia is likely to continue taking a large share of global foreign direct investment flows, says HSBC economists in a commentary. While the Trump administration's demand for investment commitments from its trading partners could pull flows away from Southeast Asia, the region's economic competitiveness and Chinese businesses' need to diversify should still underpin the strong FDI flows, they say. They expect Southeast Asian economies to keep pursuing free-trade agreements, and their growing consumer markets and digital economies could offer investment opportunities beyond manufacturing. The momentum of outbound investment from China should also remain robust, with Southeast Asia likely to benefit, partly due to the region's geographic significance in China's Belt and Road Initiative, they add. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0556 GMT - The European Central Bank's staff projections at next week's policy meeting will factor in the recent trade deal with the U.S., but revisions are likely to be small versus the June forecasts, SEB Research analysts say in a note. The ECB is currently in no hurry to cut interest rates. December is expected to be the next checkpoint for a possible rate cut, with additional data and another fresh set of staff projections available by then, the analysts say. Money markets expect the ECB to keep interest rates on hold next week, leaving the deposit rate at 2.00%, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0555 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields fall in Asian trade, extending the previous day's move, as market participants await nonfarm payrolls data at 1230 GMT. The data could add to signs of labor market weakening, thus cementing expectations of an interest-rate cut by the Federal Reserve this month. "Markets should be prepared for a softer payrolls report," says Commerzank Research's Christoph Rieger in a note. Analysts in the Wall Street Journal's poll expect job additions of 75,000 in August, compared with 73,000 in July. The two-year Treasury yield falls 0.9 basis point to 3.582%; the 10-year yield falls 2.1 basis points to 4.154% and the 30-year yield drops 3 basis points to 3.842%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0547 GMT - Ultralong Japanese government bond yields are expected to decline, but this may require additional action by the Ministry of Finance, Citi's Tomohisa Fujiki says in a note. "While the MoF considering a reduction in liquidity tap auctions for bonds with a maturity of longer than 15.5 years should provide temporary support, we think buyback measures are still required to generate new demand for off-the-run bonds," the strategist says. The yield on the 30-year bond remains elevated but had been trading within a relatively narrow range, he notes. However, selling has accelerated due to developments within the Liberal Democratic Party, the potential for expansionary fiscal policy and upward pressure on rates in Europe. The 30-year JGB yield falls 3 bps to 3.232%, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0522 GMT - China could use more consumption-based stimulus rather than investment-related stimulus to kick-start the economy after choppy July data, say HSBC economist Frederic Neumann and associate Abanti Bhaumik. "After years and years of rapid investment, the argument goes, any further boost would merely exacerbate structural imbalances and, in many areas, create yet more excess capacity," they say in a note. Even then, China's productivity gains from its investments remains decent compared with global peers. While that's not the case for all segments, outside of the property sector, "China has still plenty of potential. Even if any stimulus is more investment than consumption-led, we'll still take it," they say. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0403 GMT - The Philippines' strong inflation momentum in August is unlikely to last, HSBC Asean economist Aris Dacanay writes in a research report. Headline CPI rose 1.5% on year, due to a steep uptick in vegetable prices. "Once supply conditions for food stabilize, the momentum seen in both headline and core CPI will likely wane," Dacanay says. Although the latest data increases the risk of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas holding on to its monetary reins, HSBC reckons that there's still the possibility of a rate cut. HSBC continues to expect the central bank to deliver one more rate cut by the end of the year. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0328 GMT - Taiwan's consumer-price inflation likely stayed subdued at 1.5% on year in August, according to the median forecast of six economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. Taiwan's inflation has been under control in recent months, coming in at 1.54% in July and 1.37% in June, well below the policymakers' 2% target. The island's consumer prices could face upward pressure due to widespread price hikes in vegetables in early August from typhoons, Deutsche Bank analysts said in a note. Despite the moderate inflation pressure, ING economists expect Taiwan's central bank to remain on hold at its September meeting. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0325 GMT - The Singapore dollar strengthens against its U.S. counterpart in the Asian session. Markets will watch the U.S. nonfarm payrolls report due later today, DBS Group Research's Chang Wei Liang writes in a commentary. Another weak print will solidify expectations of a Fed rate cut later this month, says the forex and credit strategist. "The USD may slip broadly as markets price in a more dovish-leaning Fed," he adds. USD/SGD is 0.2% lower at 1.2870, LSEG data shows.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)
